heroOverview: Calcium pyrophosphate deposition (CPPD) disease is an inflammatory arthritis caused by calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate crystals depositing inside joint cartilage and synovial fluid. Prevalence rises sharply with age: chondrocalcinosis is detectable on radiograph in roughly 7% of adults aged 60-69 and over 20% of those aged 80 or older. Acute attacks — historically called pseudogout — mimic gout, producing sudden severe joint swelling, warmth, and pain that peaks within 24-48 hours. The knee is involved in at least 50% of acute episodes. Without management, attacks recur and some patients develop chronic CPPD arthropathy resembling osteoarthritis or rheumatoid arthritis. Anti-inflammatory drugs relieve acute flares; no approved disease-modifying therapy yet eliminates crystal deposits.

keyStats.prevalence: Radiographic chondrocalcinosis in 7% of adults aged 60-69, rising to 21-27% above age 80 (Neogi et al., Arthritis Rheum 2011)
keyStats.demographics: Men and women equally affected overall; women predominate in older-onset disease; male predominance in early-onset familial forms
keyStats.avgAge: Acute pseudogout attacks typically begin after age 60; rare familial forms can present in the 30s-40s
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 3-4 million symptomatic CPPD patients in the United States; exact global prevalence under-studied

definition: CPPD disease (ICD-10: M11.2) is an arthropathy caused by the formation and deposition of calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate (CPP) crystals in articular cartilage, fibrocartilage, tendons, and synovial fluid. Formerly classified under the umbrella term chondrocalcinosis, the 2011 European League Against Rheumatism (EULAR) task force refined nomenclature to distinguish three distinct clinical presentations: acute CPP crystal arthritis (pseudogout), chronic CPP crystal inflammatory arthritis, and osteoarthritis with CPP crystals. The underlying mechanism involves elevated inorganic pyrophosphate in the joint microenvironment, driven by dysregulated activity of the enzyme ENPP1 and the pyrophosphate transporter ANKH. Crystal deposition triggers phagocytosis by synovial neutrophils, activating the NLRP3 inflammasome and releasing IL-1β — the same pathway activated in gout. Unlike monosodium urate crystals in gout, CPP crystals are positively birefringent under compensated polarized light. CPPD predominantly affects larger joints (knee, wrist, hip, shoulder, ankle, pubic symphysis) rather than the first metatarsophalangeal joint typical of gout. The condition is managed primarily by rheumatologists, orthopedic surgeons, and general internists, and secondary causes (hyperparathyroidism, hemochromatosis, hypomagnesemia, hypophosphatasia) must be excluded in patients under 60.

typesClassification.description: Sudden-onset monoarthritis or oligoarthritis with joint effusion, erythema, warmth, and severe pain, typically peaking within 24-48 hours and resolving over 1-3 weeks even without treatment. Precipitated by intercurrent illness, surgery, or trauma.

Persistent symmetrical polyarthritis with morning stiffness lasting more than 45 minutes, elevated CRP and ESR, and radiographic CPP deposits. Can mimic rheumatoid arthritis; rheumatoid factor is typically negative.
CPP deposition superimposed on osteoarthritic joints, often with more inflammatory flares, more rapid cartilage loss, and heavier subchondral change than OA without crystals.
Radiographic calcification of cartilage without symptoms. Increasingly common with age; prevalence roughly doubles each decade after age 60.

Early-onset CPPD before age 55 with polyarticular involvement and a first-degree relative with similar disease. Associated with mutations in ANKH, ENPP1, or CIAS1; autosomal dominant in most kindreds.

primarySymptoms: Sudden severe pain in a large joint — most often the knee — that peaks within 24-48 hours and is accompanied by warmth, swelling, and erythema over the joint.
Large joint effusion producing visible swelling and a palpable fluid-filled cavity, most conspicuous at the knee (ballottement sign).
Restricted range of motion during an acute attack due to pain and effusion; the joint is held in a position of comfort, usually slight flexion.
Self-limiting acute attacks lasting 1-3 weeks if untreated; complete resolution between episodes in early disease.
Bilateral wrist pain with swelling and tenderness over the radiocarpal joint, a characteristic site that helps distinguish pseudogout from gout.
Shoulder pain and reduced abduction from CPP deposition in the rotator cuff or supraspinatus tendon, sometimes presenting as Milwaukee shoulder syndrome with rapid glenohumeral destruction.
Morning stiffness lasting more than 45 minutes in patients with chronic CPP crystal inflammatory arthritis, resembling rheumatoid arthritis.
Neck pain and stiffness from CPP deposition in the odontoid process (crowned dens syndrome), which can cause an acute pain crisis mimicking meningitis or cervical fracture.
Low-grade persistent joint pain and crepitus in affected joints between acute flares in patients with chronic OA-CPPD overlap.
earlyWarningSigns: Chondrocalcinosis visible on plain radiograph (calcification of the knee menisci, triangular fibrocartilage of the wrist, or symphysis pubis) in a patient over 50 with joint pain — indicates elevated crystal load before the first acute attack.
Unexplained monoarthritis with a large joint effusion in a patient over 60, even in the absence of fever, because septic arthritis and pseudogout both require urgent joint aspiration.
Neck stiffness and fever in a patient with known CPPD or chondrocalcinosis — crowned dens syndrome can mimic meningitis and should prompt CT of the upper cervical spine.
Recurrent acute monoarthritis in a patient under 55 without gout risk factors — consider secondary causes (hyperparathyroidism, hemochromatosis) and family history of early arthritis.
emergencySigns: Acute monoarthritis with fever above 38°C and systemic signs — must be treated as septic arthritis until joint aspirate and culture prove otherwise, as untreated septic arthritis causes irreversible joint destruction within 24-72 hours.
Sudden severe neck pain, torticollis, and fever in a patient with crown dens syndrome — cervical cord compromise is rare but requires urgent CT cervical spine.
Rapidly destructive shoulder arthropathy with large bloody effusions (Milwaukee shoulder) progressing over weeks — requires urgent orthopedic assessment to preserve glenohumeral function.

causes.cause: Dysregulated pyrophosphate metabolism in articular cartilage
causes.description: Elevated inorganic pyrophosphate (PPi) in synovial fluid results from increased production by chondrocytes via ENPP1 and impaired clearance via reduced alkaline phosphatase activity. When PPi concentrations exceed the solubility threshold, CPP crystals nucleate within cartilage matrix. This idiopathic mechanism accounts for the large majority of age-related CPPD.

Elevated parathyroid hormone increases calcium flux through chondrocytes, raising intracellular calcium and promoting CPP crystal nucleation. CPPD prevalence in primary hyperparathyroidism is 20-30%, and CPPD onset before age 55 should prompt serum calcium and PTH measurement.

Iron overload inhibits pyrophosphatase activity, allowing PPi to accumulate. CPPD with predominant metacarpophalangeal joint involvement, chondrocalcinosis, and elevated ferritin in a middle-aged person should trigger HFE gene testing and transferrin saturation measurement.

Magnesium acts as a co-factor for pyrophosphatase; low serum magnesium impairs PPi catabolism, promoting crystal deposition. Causes include Gitelman syndrome, diuretic use, alcohol excess, and malabsorption.

Deficient tissue non-specific alkaline phosphatase (encoded by ALPL) leads to PPi accumulation and early-onset CPPD. Adult hypophosphatasia should be considered in any patient with CPPD under 50 with stress fractures or low alkaline phosphatase.

Autosomal dominant mutations in ANKH (pyrophosphate transporter) and ENPP1 (ectonucleotide pyrophosphatase) produce familial CPPD with polyarticular onset before age 55. These mutations elevate extracellular PPi directly through altered transport or synthesis.

riskFactors.description: The single strongest risk factor. Chondrocalcinosis prevalence rises from ~7% at age 60-69 to over 21% above age 80, likely reflecting cumulative cartilage degeneration and reduced chondrocyte PPi clearance.
Mechanical cartilage damage, prior meniscectomy, and joint surgery promote local PPi release. Post-traumatic CPPD can appear within months of injury.
Present in up to 30% of patients with CPPD onset under age 60. Parathyroidectomy can reduce flare frequency, though crystal deposits do not fully resolve.
Phlebotomy reducing iron stores often does not reverse CPPD but may reduce flare frequency. Diagnosis changes overall management and family screening.
Thiazide and loop diuretics reduce renal magnesium reabsorption. Correcting hypomagnesemia with oral magnesium supplementation can reduce CPP crystal load in some patients.
First-degree relative with CPPD before age 55 raises clinical suspicion for ANKH or ENPP1 mutation. Familial forms tend to be polyarticular and more destructive.
Hypothyroidism, Bartter syndrome, and acromegaly are associated with CPPD at rates exceeding general-population prevalence; thyroid function should be checked in new-onset CPPD.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 60 represent the vast majority of CPPD cases; prevalence approximately doubles with each additional decade of age.
Men and women are equally affected overall, but familial early-onset CPPD more often presents in men in the reported kindreds.
Patients with secondary metabolic causes (hyperparathyroidism, hemochromatosis) typically present in their 40s-50s, often a decade before idiopathic age-related CPPD.
No clear ethnic predisposition identified for idiopathic CPPD, though registry data are sparse in non-European populations.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of CPPD disease rests on three pillars: synovial fluid crystal analysis, imaging, and clinical context. The gold standard remains polarized-light microscopy of aspirated synovial fluid: CPP crystals appear as weakly positively birefringent rhomboid or rod-shaped crystals, distinguishing them from the needle-shaped negatively birefringent monosodium urate crystals of gout. During an acute attack, plain radiographs often show chondrocalcinosis — calcification of the knee menisci, the triangular fibrocartilage complex of the wrist, the symphysis pubis, or the glenohumeral labrum — which supports but does not confirm CPPD, as not all CPP-positive patients show radiographic change. Musculoskeletal ultrasound has become increasingly important: CPP deposits produce a characteristic hyperechoic band within the substance of hyaline cartilage (mid-layer calcification), as opposed to gout tophi which coat the cartilage surface. Dual-energy CT can detect CPP deposits with high sensitivity but is not routinely needed. Serum tests are essential to exclude secondary causes, particularly in patients under 60: serum calcium, phosphate, alkaline phosphatase, PTH, ferritin, transferrin saturation, and magnesium. Elevated serum calcium with elevated or non-suppressed PTH points to primary hyperparathyroidism; elevated ferritin with elevated transferrin saturation prompts HFE gene testing; low alkaline phosphatase raises the possibility of hypophosphatasia. ACR 2023 classification criteria for CPPD require positive synovial fluid crystal analysis or characteristic imaging findings with compatible clinical presentation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard identification of CPP crystals confirming acute or chronic CPPD
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A needle is inserted into the swollen joint under aseptic technique to withdraw fluid. The fluid is analyzed immediately by a trained microscopist. CPP crystals appear weakly positively birefringent under compensated polarized light. Results are usually available within 1-2 hours if done in-house.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates chondrocalcinosis (calcified cartilage) supporting CPPD diagnosis and assessing joint damage
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard X-ray of the affected joint, taking under five minutes. Chondrocalcinosis appears as fine calcification within the fibrocartilage (menisci, triangular fibrocartilage) or hyaline cartilage. A normal X-ray does not exclude CPPD.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects CPP crystal deposits in cartilage and distinguishes them from gout tophi; guides joint aspiration
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Gel is applied and a probe is moved over the joint. The examination takes 10-20 minutes. Mid-layer cartilage hyperechogenicity indicates CPP; surface hyperechogenicity indicates monosodium urate. No radiation; can be done during the acute flare.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum metabolic panel (calcium, phosphate, ALP, PTH, ferritin, transferrin saturation, magnesium, TSH)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for secondary metabolic causes of CPPD, especially in patients under 60
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draws. Results return within 24 hours in most settings. Abnormal results guide secondary cause workup and genetic testing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Highly sensitive detection of CPP deposits when plain X-ray and ultrasound are inconclusive
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Short CT scan of the affected region, usually 10 minutes. DECT algorithms color-code different crystal types. Not routinely required but helpful in atypical or diagnostic uncertainty cases.

diagnosticTests.test: Synovial fluid Gram stain and culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes concurrent septic arthritis in any acute monoarthritis with fever or systemic signs
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Part of the same joint aspiration sample sent for microbiology. Result takes 24-72 hours for culture. CPPD and septic arthritis can coexist, so culture is mandatory when infection is a concern.

treatmentOverview: No approved therapy removes CPP crystal deposits or prevents their formation; all current treatment is directed at managing acute flares and reducing chronic inflammation. For acute CPP crystal arthritis, the 2021 EULAR recommendations endorse joint aspiration and intra-articular corticosteroid injection as the most effective acute intervention, providing relief within 24 hours by directly removing inflammatory cytokines and delivering anti-inflammatory drug to the site. Oral colchicine (0.5 mg two to three times daily for 3-7 days) or low-dose NSAIDs (e.g., naproxen 500 mg twice daily) are equivalent first-line oral options for patients without contraindications. A short course of oral prednisolone (30-35 mg/day for 5 days) is preferred when renal impairment or gastrointestinal disease precludes both options. For chronic CPP crystal inflammatory arthritis causing frequent flares or persistent synovitis, low-dose daily colchicine (0.5-1 mg/day) reduces flare frequency by approximately 40% in short-term randomized trials. Hydroxychloroquine (200-400 mg/day) and low-dose NSAIDs have been used off-label with modest evidence. IL-1 inhibitors (anakinra, canakinumab) are effective in refractory or recurrent acute attacks based on case series and small trials; this approach is not yet formally licensed for CPPD but is supported by EULAR as a treatment option. When CPPD overlaps with severe osteoarthritis producing mechanical symptoms, joint replacement surgery provides durable pain relief and functional improvement, though crystal deposits do not disappear. Treating secondary causes (parathyroidectomy for hyperparathyroidism, phlebotomy for hemochromatosis, magnesium supplementation for hypomagnesemia) may reduce future crystal load but does not reliably resolve established deposits.

medicalTreatments.name: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone acetonide 40 mg or methylprednisolone 40-80 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line acute treatment: joint is aspirated, then steroid injected. Fastest onset, especially effective in single-joint attacks. Repeated injection at intervals of at least 3 months is used for recurrent disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Over 80% of patients experience significant pain relief within 24-48 hours; comparable to NSAIDs in head-to-head data

medicalTreatments.name: Colchicine (0.5 mg two to three times daily for 3-7 days; prophylactic 0.5-1 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-inflammatory alkaloid that inhibits neutrophil migration and NLRP3 inflammasome activation. Used both for acute flares and as long-term prophylaxis. Dose-reduce in renal impairment and with CYP3A4 inhibitors.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Acute flare resolution in 60-70% within 48-72 hours; long-term prophylaxis reduces flare frequency by ~40% in trials

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (naproxen 500 mg twice daily or indomethacin 50 mg three times daily for 5-7 days)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line oral option for acute pseudogout in patients with adequate renal and gastrointestinal function. Use with a proton-pump inhibitor in those at GI risk. Avoid in CKD, heart failure, and concurrent anticoagulation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates similar to colchicine; symptom improvement typically within 24-48 hours

medicalTreatments.name: Oral prednisolone (30-35 mg/day for 5-7 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Preferred when NSAIDs and colchicine are contraindicated (CKD, GI disease, drug interactions). Taper not required for short courses. Monitor blood glucose in diabetic patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable efficacy to NSAIDs in acute crystal arthritis based on randomized trial data

medicalTreatments.name: IL-1 inhibitors (anakinra 100 mg subcutaneous daily for 3-5 days; canakinumab 150 mg subcutaneous once)
medicalTreatments.description: Used off-label for refractory acute attacks or frequent flares in patients who cannot tolerate or do not respond to standard anti-inflammatories. Target the NLRP3 inflammasome–IL-1β axis central to CPP crystal inflammation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid flare resolution in 70-90% of refractory cases in observational studies; randomized trial evidence limited

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxychloroquine (200-400 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Off-label long-term option for chronic CPP crystal inflammatory arthritis resembling RA. Inhibits crystal-induced lysosomal enzyme release. Requires annual ophthalmologic monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest reduction in flare frequency and joint tenderness in open-label studies; no randomized controlled trial data yet

surgicalOptions.name: Total knee replacement
surgicalOptions.description: Indicated when CPPD-associated osteoarthritis produces disabling pain and functional limitation refractory to medical management. Crystal deposits within the joint do not impair the outcome of the prosthesis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Over 90% patient satisfaction at 10 years; consistent with outcomes in primary OA without CPPD

surgicalOptions.name: Arthroscopic lavage
surgicalOptions.description: Irrigation of the joint with large volumes of saline removes free-floating crystals and inflammatory cytokines. Provides short-term symptom relief; durable benefit not established in randomized trials for CPPD specifically.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom benefit in 60-70% of patients at 3-6 months; evidence base for CPPD is limited to small case series

alternativeTreatments.name: Magnesium supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Oral magnesium (300-400 mg/day elemental magnesium) corrects hypomagnesemia-associated CPPD. One small randomized trial found reduced radiographic chondrocalcinosis score with magnesium citrate over 6 months in patients with documented hypomagnesemia.

whySeeSpecialist: Rheumatologist referral is appropriate when the diagnosis is uncertain (especially excluding septic arthritis or gout), when attacks are frequent (more than three per year), when chronic CPPD inflammatory arthritis resembles RA, or when secondary metabolic causes need investigation and co-management. Orthopedic referral is warranted when progressive joint damage requires surgical intervention.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a rheumatologist with expertise in crystal arthropathies and experience with polarized-light microscopy interpretation. For patients under 55, a rheumatologist with metabolic bone and endocrine co-management experience is ideal. For surgical candidates, seek an orthopedic surgeon with high-volume joint replacement experience.
hospitalCriteria: Ability to perform same-day synovial fluid polarized-light microscopy for acute monoarthritis
Rheumatology outpatient service with ultrasound-guided joint injection capability
Access to metabolic and endocrine specialists for secondary cause workup
Orthopedic surgery service for joint replacement in advanced disease
keyFacilities: Rheumatology units with in-house synovial fluid analysis
Academic medical centers with DECT imaging capability
preventionStrategies: Identify and treat secondary causes early (hyperparathyroidism, hemochromatosis, hypomagnesemia) to reduce ongoing crystal deposition, particularly in patients presenting under age 60.
Maintain adequate hydration and avoid prolonged joint immobilization, which can precipitate acute flares after surgery or illness.
Correct magnesium deficiency with oral supplementation when hypomagnesemia is documented as the underlying driver.
Prophylactic low-dose colchicine (0.5-1 mg daily) after parathyroid surgery or major joint surgery can reduce perioperative pseudogout flares.
Avoid unnecessary joint trauma and implement strategies to reduce joint injury risk in occupational or high-impact settings.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain healthy body weight to reduce mechanical load on affected joints (particularly the knees), which is linked to accelerated cartilage loss when CPP deposits are present.
Engage in low-impact aerobic exercise (swimming, cycling) between flares to preserve joint function and muscle strength without provoking crystal release.
Limit alcohol intake, which worsens hypomagnesemia and can precipitate acute flares.
Follow a balanced diet adequate in magnesium (nuts, seeds, leafy greens, legumes) and limit fructose-heavy foods that reduce renal magnesium reabsorption.
Work with a physical therapist on joint-protective movement patterns and assistive devices to reduce impact loading on affected large joints.
Rest and elevate the affected joint during acute attacks; ice packs (wrapped in cloth) applied for 15-20 minutes can reduce local inflammation.

recommended: Magnesium-rich foods (almonds, pumpkin seeds, dark chocolate, avocado, spinach) to support pyrophosphatase activity
Calcium from dairy or fortified plant foods within normal daily requirements — excessive calcium restriction is not beneficial and may worsen bone health
Anti-inflammatory foods (fatty fish, olive oil, berries) to reduce systemic background inflammation
Adequate fluid intake (at least 2 L/day) to support renal clearance of metabolic byproducts
avoid: Excessive alcohol, which lowers serum magnesium and can precipitate flares
High-fructose corn syrup and sweetened beverages, which impair renal magnesium reabsorption
Very high-dose vitamin D or calcium supplements without medical supervision, which could worsen hypercalcemia in secondary hyperparathyroidism
Prolonged fasting or crash diets, which can trigger metabolic shifts precipitating acute crystal flares

exerciseGuidelines: During acute flares, rest the affected joint and avoid weight-bearing activity. Between attacks, low-impact aerobic activity (swimming, cycling, walking) 150 minutes per week is recommended to maintain joint mobility and reduce inflammation. Strengthening exercises for the quadriceps and hip stabilizers reduce knee joint loading. Avoid high-impact activities (running on hard surfaces, jump training) if the knees are heavily involved.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed anti-inflammatory medications consistently at the first sign of a flare to abort the attack early; do not wait until pain is maximal.
Track flare frequency, joints affected, and duration in a simple diary to identify triggers and assist medication titration at rheumatology visits.
Monitor renal function and CBC every 6-12 months if on long-term colchicine; reduce dose when eGFR falls below 50 mL/min/1.73m².
Keep a short-course rescue pack (oral prednisolone or colchicine) available for weekends and travel when specialist access is limited.
Attend annual rheumatology reviews with repeat imaging of the most affected joint to track crystal load and structural progression.
prognosis: The prognosis of CPPD disease is generally favorable for acute attacks, which are self-limiting and respond well to anti-inflammatory therapy. Recurrence is common: roughly 50% of patients with a first acute episode experience another within 2 years. Patients with frequent attacks (more than three per year) and chronic CPP crystal inflammatory arthritis face progressive joint damage comparable to erosive osteoarthritis, with significant functional impairment in 15-20% of cases over a 10-year horizon. Rapidly destructive arthropathy (Milwaukee shoulder syndrome) is rare but can lead to complete glenohumeral destruction within 12-18 months. Secondary CPPD from treatable causes (hyperparathyroidism, hemochromatosis) carries a favorable outlook when the underlying cause is addressed, though established crystal deposits rarely disappear entirely. Life expectancy is not significantly shortened by CPPD, but the impact on mobility, sleep, and quality of life from recurrent flares can be substantial in older patients with limited physiologic reserve.
recoveryTimeline: An acute pseudogout attack resolves within 1-3 weeks with treatment; aspiration and intra-articular steroid typically produce marked improvement within 24-48 hours. Oral NSAIDs or colchicine usually control flares within 3-7 days. Chronic CPPD inflammatory arthritis responds more slowly to low-dose colchicine or hydroxychloroquine over 6-12 weeks. Joint damage from long-standing disease does not reverse with medical therapy alone.
complications: Rapidly destructive large joint arthropathy (Milwaukee shoulder) with complete rotator-cuff destruction, massive glenohumeral effusions, and joint instability developing over 12-24 months — regular shoulder ultrasound in patients with recurrent shoulder pseudogout.

Crowned dens syndrome from CPP deposits in the transverse atlantal ligament and C2 facet joints, causing acute severe neck pain, torticollis, and fever; exclude meningitis and cervical fracture with CT and lumbar puncture.
Secondary osteoarthritis with accelerated cartilage loss and osteophyte formation in joints bearing heavy CPP deposits, particularly the knee and wrist.
Functional dependency and falls risk in older patients with frequent flares affecting weight-bearing joints, especially when polypharmacy limits anti-inflammatory options.
Misdiagnosis as rheumatoid arthritis leading to unnecessary immunosuppressive therapy, particularly in chronic polyarticular CPPD with elevated inflammatory markers.

supportResources.name: Arthritis Foundation — Pseudogout
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of CPPD, flare management tips, and finding a rheumatologist.

supportResources.name: EULAR — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: European rheumatology patient information including crystal arthritis.

supportResources.name: American College of Rheumatology — CPPD
supportResources.description: ACR patient education page covering symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment of CPPD disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gout
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause sudden acute monoarthritis with joint swelling and erythema, but gout predominantly attacks the first metatarsophalangeal joint (podagra) and is confirmed by negatively birefringent needle-shaped monosodium urate crystals on polarized microscopy. CPPD attacks the knee and wrist more often and shows weakly positively birefringent rhomboid crystals. Serum uric acid may be normal during a gout flare and is not elevated in CPPD.

confusedWithConditions.name: Osteoarthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: OA produces gradual-onset joint pain and stiffness worsened by activity, whereas CPPD produces acute inflammatory flares superimposed on a background of chronic joint change. CPPD crystals are absent in pure OA. Radiographic chondrocalcinosis in a patient with OA-type X-ray changes suggests CPPD-OA overlap.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic CPPD crystal inflammatory arthritis can mimic RA with symmetrical small and large joint involvement and elevated ESR/CRP. However, RA typically involves MCP and PIP joints with marginal erosions and shows positive RF or anti-CCP in 70-80% of cases. CPPD shows radiographic chondrocalcinosis, negative RF, and crystal analysis confirms the diagnosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Reactive Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Reactive arthritis follows urogenital or enteric infection and typically affects young adults with asymmetric lower-limb oligoarthritis, enthesitis, and sometimes mucocutaneous lesions. CPPD predominates in older adults without a preceding infection trigger; crystal analysis and imaging differentiate them.

relatedConditions.name: Gout
relatedConditions.relevance: The other major crystal arthropathy; frequently in the differential and can co-occur in the same patient (mixed crystal deposition).

relatedConditions.name: Osteoarthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: CPP crystals commonly deposit in osteoarthritic joints, accelerating cartilage loss and producing an OA-CPPD overlap phenotype.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic CPPD arthritis mimics RA and must be distinguished before initiating biologic DMARDs.

relatedConditions.name: Ankylosing Spondylitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another inflammatory arthritis causing axial and peripheral joint involvement; differentiated by HLA-B27, SI joint changes, and absence of crystal deposits.

faqs.question: What is CPPD disease?
faqs.answer: CPPD disease is an arthritis caused by calcium pyrophosphate crystals depositing in joint cartilage and fluid. The crystals trigger sudden painful flares called pseudogout, most often in the knee or wrist. CPPD is most common after age 60 and cannot yet be cured, but flares respond well to anti-inflammatory medications.

faqs.question: What is pseudogout?
faqs.answer: Pseudogout is the acute flare form of CPPD disease. Calcium pyrophosphate crystals shed into the joint cavity cause sudden severe pain, swelling, warmth, and redness peaking within 24-48 hours. The knee is affected in at least 50% of cases. Despite the name, pseudogout is caused by different crystals than true gout.

faqs.question: How is CPPD different from gout?
faqs.answer: Gout is caused by monosodium urate crystals (negatively birefringent needles) and most often attacks the big toe. CPPD uses calcium pyrophosphate crystals (weakly positively birefringent rhomboids) and typically strikes the knee or wrist. Serum uric acid is elevated in gout but normal in CPPD. Both are confirmed by polarized-light microscopy of joint fluid.

faqs.question: What causes CPPD disease?
faqs.answer: CPPD results from elevated pyrophosphate in joint fluid, allowing calcium pyrophosphate crystals to form. Most cases are idiopathic and age-related. Secondary causes in younger patients include primary hyperparathyroidism, hemochromatosis, and hypomagnesemia. Genetic mutations in ANKH or ENPP1 cause rare familial early-onset disease.

faqs.question: Which joints does CPPD most commonly affect?
faqs.answer: The knee is the most commonly affected joint, accounting for over 50% of acute pseudogout attacks. Other frequent sites include the wrist, ankle, shoulder, elbow, and hip. The first metatarsophalangeal joint (big toe) is rarely affected, which helps distinguish CPPD from gout.

faqs.question: How is CPPD diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Definitive diagnosis requires polarized-light microscopy of synovial fluid showing weakly positively birefringent rhomboid crystals. Plain X-rays often show chondrocalcinosis (cartilage calcification). Musculoskeletal ultrasound reveals mid-layer cartilage hyperechogenicity. Blood tests screen for secondary causes in patients under 60.

faqs.question: Is there a blood test for CPPD?
faqs.answer: No blood test directly detects CPPD. Diagnosis requires joint fluid analysis. Blood tests (serum calcium, PTH, ferritin, transferrin saturation, magnesium, alkaline phosphatase, TSH) are used to screen for secondary metabolic causes — particularly important in patients with CPPD onset before age 60.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for a pseudogout attack?
faqs.answer: Joint aspiration followed by intra-articular corticosteroid injection provides the fastest relief, typically within 24-48 hours. Oral alternatives include colchicine, NSAIDs, or short-course prednisolone. Ice, rest, and elevation support recovery. Most acute attacks resolve within 1-3 weeks with appropriate treatment.

faqs.question: Can diet changes prevent CPPD attacks?
faqs.answer: No specific diet is proven to prevent CPPD. Adequate magnesium intake (nuts, seeds, leafy greens) supports pyrophosphatase activity. Avoiding excess alcohol prevents hypomagnesemia. Maintaining healthy weight reduces mechanical stress on affected joints. These steps may reduce flare frequency but cannot eliminate crystal deposits.

faqs.question: Does colchicine work for CPPD?
faqs.answer: Yes. Colchicine is a first-line option for both acute pseudogout (0.5 mg two to three times daily for up to 7 days) and long-term flare prevention (0.5-1 mg daily). It reduces flare frequency by approximately 40% in short-term randomized trials. Dose reduction is required when kidney function is impaired.

faqs.question: Can CPPD be cured?
faqs.answer: No approved therapy dissolves CPP crystals or prevents new deposits. Treatment targets inflammation during flares and reduces their frequency. Treating secondary causes such as hyperparathyroidism or hypomagnesemia may slow further crystal accumulation. Joint replacement can restore function in severely damaged joints.

faqs.question: Is CPPD hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most CPPD is idiopathic and age-related. Familial early-onset CPPD occurs in patients under 55 with autosomal dominant mutations in ANKH or ENPP1 genes. A first-degree relative with CPPD before age 55 warrants genetic counseling and earlier metabolic screening.

faqs.question: What is chondrocalcinosis?
faqs.answer: Chondrocalcinosis is calcification of joint cartilage visible on X-ray, caused by calcium crystal deposition — most often CPP crystals. It is detectable in about 7% of adults aged 60-69 and over 20% above age 80. Chondrocalcinosis alone does not confirm symptomatic CPPD; crystal analysis of joint fluid is needed for diagnosis.

faqs.question: Can CPPD affect the spine?
faqs.answer: Yes. CPP crystals can deposit in the atlanto-axial joint and transverse ligament of the cervical spine, causing crowned dens syndrome — acute severe neck pain, torticollis, and fever that can mimic meningitis. CT of the upper cervical spine confirms the diagnosis. This presentation requires urgent assessment.

faqs.question: How often do pseudogout attacks recur?
faqs.answer: About 50% of patients experience a second attack within 2 years of the first. Recurrence is more frequent in patients with widespread chondrocalcinosis, secondary metabolic causes, or poor control of underlying disease. Long-term prophylaxis with colchicine reduces attack frequency in patients with three or more flares per year.

symptomKeywords: joint swelling that looks like gout but not big toe
metaDescription: CPPD disease (pseudogout): calcium crystal arthritis causing sudden knee or wrist pain, common after 60. Diagnosis, treatment, and flare prevention reviewed.
